nodes.title: Ad Hominem
nodes.oneLiner: Attacking the person making the argument instead of the argument itself.

nodes.title: Affect Heuristic
nodes.oneLiner: When your feelings about something shape what you believe to be true about it.

nodes.title: Alienation
nodes.oneLiner: The feeling of being disconnected from your work, from other people, and from yourself by the structures you live within.

nodes.title: Anchoring Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The first piece of information you encounter disproportionately shapes everything that follows.

nodes.title: Appeal to Emotion
nodes.oneLiner: Using feelings rather than evidence to persuade - bypassing the argument and going straight for the heart.

nodes.title: Appeal to False Authority
nodes.oneLiner: Using an expert's opinion as evidence when they have no relevant expertise - fame and credentials aren't the same thing.

nodes.title: Appeal to Nature
nodes.oneLiner: Arguing that something is good because it's natural, or bad because it's artificial - as though nature is always benign.

nodes.title: Authority Bias
nodes.oneLiner: We give disproportionate weight to the opinions of people we perceive as authorities - even outside their expertise.

nodes.title: Availability Heuristic
nodes.oneLiner: We judge how likely something is based on how easily we can think of an example - not on how often it actually happens.

nodes.title: Aversive Racism
nodes.oneLiner: When people who genuinely believe they are not prejudiced still harbour unconscious biases that shape their behaviour in subtle ways.

nodes.title: Backfire Effect
nodes.oneLiner: When correcting someone's false belief makes them believe it even more strongly.

nodes.title: Bandwagon Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency to adopt beliefs, behaviours, or trends because other people are doing so.

nodes.title: Ben Franklin Effect
nodes.oneLiner: We grow to like people we've done favours for, not just people who've done favours for us.

nodes.title: Betrayal Aversion
nodes.oneLiner: We'd rather face a worse outcome from chance than a better one that carries any risk of being betrayed by another person.

nodes.title: Blind Spot Bias
nodes.oneLiner: Recognising cognitive biases in others while failing to see them in yourself.

nodes.title: Bread and Circuses
nodes.oneLiner: Keeping a population content through entertainment and material comfort so they don't question the systems they live under.

nodes.title: Burden of Proof
nodes.oneLiner: The obligation to provide evidence rests with the person making the claim - not with the person questioning it.

nodes.title: Bystander Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The more people who witness a problem, the less likely any one of them is to help.

nodes.title: Capitalist Realism
nodes.oneLiner: The pervasive sense that capitalism is the only viable system - not because it's the best, but because alternatives have become unthinkable.

nodes.title: Circle of Competence
nodes.oneLiner: Knowing the boundaries of what you genuinely understand - and having the discipline to stay inside them when it matters.

nodes.title: Circular Reasoning
nodes.oneLiner: An argument that uses its own conclusion as one of its premises - going round in circles without proving anything.

nodes.title: Cognitive Dissonance
nodes.oneLiner: The uncomfortable tension we feel when holding two contradictory beliefs at the same time.

nodes.title: Collective Amnesia
nodes.oneLiner: How societies forget inconvenient histories, allowing harmful patterns to repeat unchallenged.

nodes.title: Commodification
nodes.oneLiner: The process of turning things that aren't naturally products - ideas, identity, relationships, rest - into things that can be bought and sold.

nodes.title: Compartmentalisation
nodes.oneLiner: Keeping contradictory beliefs, values, or behaviours in separate mental boxes so they never have to confront each other.

nodes.title: Compassion Fatigue
nodes.oneLiner: When constant exposure to suffering erodes your ability to care, not because you're heartless but because you're human.

nodes.title: Conceptual Gentrification
nodes.oneLiner: When ideas are stripped of their challenging, uncomfortable, or radical parts and repackaged for comfortable mainstream consumption.

nodes.title: Concern Trolling
nodes.oneLiner: Disguising hostile opposition as caring advice to undermine a cause from within.

nodes.title: Confirmation Bias
nodes.oneLiner: We seek out information that supports what we already believe, and ignore what doesn't.

nodes.title: Contrast Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency for your judgement of something to shift depending on what you compare it to.

nodes.title: Cultural Defaults
nodes.oneLiner: The unexamined norms we follow simply because they're treated as standard.

nodes.title: Cultural Hegemony
nodes.oneLiner: When the dominant group's ideas become everyone's 'common sense' - accepted as natural rather than constructed.

nodes.title: DARVO
nodes.oneLiner: A manipulation pattern where the offender denies wrongdoing, attacks the accuser, and reverses victim and offender roles.

nodes.title: Denial
nodes.oneLiner: The refusal to accept an uncomfortable truth, even when the evidence is overwhelming.

nodes.title: Diffusion of Responsibility
nodes.oneLiner: The more people who could act, the less likely any one person is to do so.

nodes.title: Dog Whistling
nodes.oneLiner: Using coded language that sounds innocent to most people but carries a hidden message to a specific audience.

nodes.title: Dunning-Kruger Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The less you know about something, the more confident you're likely to feel about it.

nodes.title: Effort Justification
nodes.oneLiner: The harder we work for something, the more we convince ourselves it was worth it - regardless of whether it was.

nodes.title: Emergence
nodes.oneLiner: Complex behaviours arising from simple rules, with no central plan or control.

nodes.title: Euphemism
nodes.oneLiner: Softening harsh realities with gentler language - sometimes kindly, sometimes to hide the truth.

nodes.title: Expectancy Violation
nodes.oneLiner: When someone breaks from expected behaviour, you don't just notice - you react more strongly than the behaviour itself would normally warrant.

nodes.title: False Balance
nodes.oneLiner: Presenting two sides as equally valid when the evidence overwhelmingly supports one.

nodes.title: False Consciousness
nodes.oneLiner: Supporting systems that work against your own interests because the culture has made them feel natural and inevitable.

nodes.title: False Consensus Effect
nodes.oneLiner: We tend to assume that most people think the way we do - and we're usually wrong.

nodes.title: False Dilemma
nodes.oneLiner: Presenting only two options when more exist - forcing a choice between extremes and ignoring everything in between.

nodes.title: False Equivalence
nodes.oneLiner: Treating two things as equally valid or important when they clearly aren't.

nodes.title: Feedback Loops
nodes.oneLiner: When the output of a system feeds back in as input, either amplifying or dampening the original effect.

nodes.title: Firehose of Falsehood
nodes.oneLiner: Overwhelming audiences with a rapid, continuous flood of disinformation so that truth becomes impossible to defend.

nodes.title: First Principles Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Breaking a problem down to its most basic truths and building up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy.

nodes.title: Framing Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The way information is presented changes how we respond to it - even when the underlying facts are identical.

nodes.title: Fundamental Attribution Error
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency to explain other people's behaviour as a result of their character while explaining your own as a result of your circumstances.

nodes.title: Gaslighting
nodes.oneLiner: Manipulating someone into doubting their own perception, memory, or sanity.

nodes.title: Gish Gallop
nodes.oneLiner: Overwhelming an opponent with a rapid flood of arguments, regardless of accuracy, so that none can be adequately addressed.

nodes.title: Halo Effect
nodes.oneLiner: One positive trait colours your entire perception of a person, product, or idea.

nodes.title: Hanlon's Razor
nodes.oneLiner: Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by ignorance, carelessness, or incompetence.

nodes.title: Hasty Generalisation
nodes.oneLiner: Drawing a broad conclusion from too few examples - treating a small sample as though it represents the whole picture.

nodes.title: Hindsight Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency to believe, after an event has occurred, that you knew it was going to happen all along.

nodes.title: Illusory Truth Effect
nodes.oneLiner: Repeat something often enough and people start to believe it - not because it's true, but because it's familiar.

nodes.title: In-Group/Out-Group Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency to favour people in your own group and view those outside it with suspicion, distrust, or hostility.

nodes.title: Independent Evaluation
nodes.oneLiner: Forming your own judgement about an idea or claim before looking at what everyone else thinks.

nodes.title: Inversion
nodes.oneLiner: Instead of asking how to succeed, ask what would guarantee failure - then avoid those things.

nodes.title: Just-World Fallacy
nodes.oneLiner: The belief that people get what they deserve - that good things happen to good people and bad things happen to bad people.

nodes.title: Learned Helplessness
nodes.oneLiner: When repeated failure teaches you to stop trying - even when the situation has changed and escape is possible.

nodes.title: Loaded Language
nodes.oneLiner: Words chosen to trigger an emotional reaction rather than communicate neutral information.

nodes.title: Loss Aversion
nodes.oneLiner: Losses feel roughly twice as painful as equivalent gains feel good.

nodes.title: Lump of Labour Fallacy
nodes.oneLiner: The mistaken belief that there is a fixed amount of work available in an economy, so one group's gain must be another's loss.

nodes.title: Manufactured Consent
nodes.oneLiner: When media systems produce public agreement with elite interests - not through censorship, but through structure.

nodes.title: Manufacturing Desire
nodes.oneLiner: The systematic creation of wants that didn't previously exist, turning luxuries into perceived necessities.

nodes.title: Map is Not the Territory
nodes.oneLiner: Every model, theory, or description of reality is a simplification - useful, but never the whole picture.

nodes.title: Mere Exposure Effect
nodes.oneLiner: We tend to prefer things simply because we've encountered them before.

nodes.title: Microaggressions
nodes.oneLiner: Small, everyday slights and indignities - often unintentional - that communicate hostility or prejudice toward members of marginalised groups.

nodes.title: Moral Hypocrisy Judgement
nodes.oneLiner: We punish the contradiction between someone's stated values and their behaviour more harshly than we punish the behaviour alone.

nodes.title: Moral Hypocrisy
nodes.oneLiner: Judging others by a stricter moral standard than the one you apply to yourself.

nodes.title: Moral Licensing
nodes.oneLiner: The psychological loophole where doing something good gives you permission to do something bad.

nodes.title: Moral Panic
nodes.oneLiner: Intense public fear about a perceived threat, amplified by media, disproportionate to the actual danger.

nodes.title: Motivated Reasoning
nodes.oneLiner: When we use reasoning not to find the truth, but to defend what we already believe.

nodes.title: Motte-and-Bailey
nodes.oneLiner: Defending a controversial claim by retreating to an uncontroversial one, then acting as if they are the same thing.

nodes.title: Moving the Goalposts
nodes.oneLiner: Changing the criteria for proof or success after they've been met - ensuring that no evidence is ever good enough.

nodes.title: Naive Realism
nodes.oneLiner: The belief that you see the world as it objectively is - and that anyone who disagrees must be biased, uninformed, or irrational.

nodes.title: Negativity Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency for negative experiences, information, and emotions to affect us more strongly than positive ones.

nodes.title: Normalcy Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The tendency to underestimate the likelihood and impact of a disaster or major disruption because things have always been fine before.

nodes.title: Normalisation
nodes.oneLiner: The gradual process by which the previously unthinkable becomes acceptable, then expected, then invisible - the slow drift of what a culture treats as normal.

nodes.title: Occam's Razor
nodes.oneLiner: When you have competing explanations for the same thing, the simplest one - the one with the fewest assumptions - is usually right.

nodes.title: Opportunity Cost
nodes.oneLiner: Every choice has a hidden price tag: the value of the next-best thing you gave up by choosing this one.

nodes.title: Overton Window
nodes.oneLiner: The range of ideas the public considers acceptable at any given time - and how that range can be deliberately shifted.

nodes.title: Paradox of Tolerance
nodes.oneLiner: A tolerant society that tolerates intolerance will eventually be destroyed by it.

nodes.title: Pluralistic Ignorance
nodes.oneLiner: When everyone privately disagrees with something but goes along with it because they assume everyone else agrees.

nodes.title: Post Hoc
nodes.oneLiner: Assuming that because one thing happened after another, the first thing caused the second - confusing sequence with causation.

nodes.title: Probabilistic Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Thinking in terms of likelihoods and ranges of outcomes rather than certainties, so you make better decisions under uncertainty.

nodes.title: Psychological Projection
nodes.oneLiner: Attributing your own uncomfortable feelings, motives, or traits to someone else.

nodes.title: Rationalisation
nodes.oneLiner: Constructing a logical-sounding explanation for a decision or behaviour that was actually driven by emotion.

nodes.title: Recuperation
nodes.oneLiner: When radical ideas are absorbed by the system they opposed and sold back as products - neutralising dissent by turning it into commerce.

nodes.title: Red Herring
nodes.oneLiner: Introducing an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the original issue.

nodes.title: Reification
nodes.oneLiner: Treating human-made ideas, systems, and social arrangements as if they were natural, fixed, and unchangeable things.

nodes.title: Relative Deprivation
nodes.oneLiner: Feeling worse off based on who you compare yourself to, not on what you actually have.

nodes.title: Scapegoating
nodes.oneLiner: Blaming a person or group for problems they didn't cause, diverting attention from the real source.

nodes.title: Sealioning
nodes.oneLiner: Disguising harassment as polite, persistent requests for evidence and debate that are never made in good faith.

nodes.title: Second-Order Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Thinking beyond the immediate consequences of a decision to consider what happens next - and what happens after that.

nodes.title: Self-Serving Bias
nodes.oneLiner: Taking credit for successes and blaming external factors for failures.

nodes.title: Slippery Slope
nodes.oneLiner: Arguing that one small step will inevitably lead to a chain of increasingly extreme consequences, without evidence that the chain is likely.

nodes.title: Social Proof
nodes.oneLiner: We look at what other people are doing to decide what we should do - especially when we're uncertain.

nodes.title: Source Laundering
nodes.oneLiner: Passing unreliable information through a chain of increasingly credible-looking sources until it appears legitimate.

nodes.title: State of Nature Assumption
nodes.oneLiner: The unconscious belief about whether people are fundamentally selfish or fundamentally cooperative - and how that shapes everything else you think.

nodes.title: Status Quo Bias
nodes.oneLiner: A preference for the way things are, even when change would be beneficial.

nodes.title: Straw Man
nodes.oneLiner: Misrepresenting someone's argument to make it easier to attack.

nodes.title: Structural Violence
nodes.oneLiner: Harm built into the design of social systems rather than inflicted by any individual act of aggression.

nodes.title: Sunk Cost Fallacy
nodes.oneLiner: Continuing to invest in something because of what you've already put in, not because of what you'll get out.

nodes.title: Survivorship Bias
nodes.oneLiner: Focusing on the people or things that succeeded while overlooking those that didn't - and drawing false conclusions from the incomplete picture.

nodes.title: Symbolic Racism
nodes.oneLiner: When prejudice is expressed not through overt hostility but through opposition to policies associated with marginalised groups, framed in the language of fairness and principle.

nodes.title: The Attention Economy
nodes.oneLiner: A system in which human attention is treated as a scarce resource to be harvested, bought, sold, and competed for - reshaping culture around whatever captures it.

nodes.title: The Culture Industry
nodes.oneLiner: When culture is mass-produced to pacify rather than challenge, turning art and entertainment into instruments of conformity.

nodes.title: The Meritocracy Myth
nodes.oneLiner: The belief that success is earned purely through talent and effort, hiding the structural advantages that shape outcomes.

nodes.title: The Spectacle
nodes.oneLiner: When life is experienced through images and representations rather than lived directly.

nodes.title: Thought-Terminating Cliche
nodes.oneLiner: A commonly used phrase that shuts down critical thinking by making further discussion feel unnecessary.

nodes.title: Tone Policing
nodes.oneLiner: Dismissing someone's argument by criticising how they expressed it rather than engaging with what they said.

nodes.title: Tragedy of the Commons
nodes.oneLiner: When individuals acting in their own rational interest collectively destroy a shared resource.

nodes.title: Unintended Consequences
nodes.oneLiner: Actions in complex systems produce outcomes nobody planned for - sometimes worse than the original problem.

nodes.title: Victim-Perpetrator Cycle
nodes.oneLiner: Groups that have suffered persecution can, once they gain power, go on to persecute others - often using their past suffering as justification.

nodes.title: Weasel Words
nodes.oneLiner: Vague qualifiers that create the impression of a meaningful claim while committing to nothing.

nodes.title: Whataboutism
nodes.oneLiner: Responding to a criticism by pointing to someone else's wrongdoing instead of addressing the original point.

nodes.title: Woozle Effect
nodes.oneLiner: When a claim gets cited so often that people assume it's been proven - even though the evidence behind it is thin or nonexistent.

nodes.title: Zero-Sum Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: The assumption that any situation is a competition where one person's gain must be another's loss.

notes.text: An ad hominem is a specific type of red herring - it diverts attention from the argument to the arguer
Ad hominem attacks can function as red herrings - attacking the person diverts attention from the argument they made.
Both avoid engaging with the actual argument - straw man distorts it, ad hominem ignores it entirely
Both avoid engaging with the actual argument - straw man distorts it, ad hominem ignores it entirely
The mirror image of ad hominem - instead of dismissing an argument because of who made it, you accept it because of who made it
Persistent ad hominem attacks can be used as a form of sealioning - wearing someone down by making the conversation about them rather than the issue
A subtler cousin - shifting focus from what someone said to how they said it
Tone policing is a form of ad hominem - attacking the speaker's manner instead of their argument.
Emotional events are more memorable, making them feel more common than they are
The availability heuristic and the affect heuristic often work together - vivid memories trigger emotional responses that shape judgement.
How information is presented shapes the emotions it triggers, which then shapes judgement
Framing works partly by triggering different emotional responses to the same information.
First emotional impressions can anchor all subsequent reasoning
Positive feelings toward a belief make us more likely to seek evidence that supports it
A rhetorical tactic that deliberately exploits the affect heuristic
The affect heuristic is the cognitive bias that makes appeal to emotion work - we judge things by how they make us feel.
Alienation from work is a direct consequence of commodification - when labour is treated as a commodity, the worker is separated from the meaning of what they produce.
Capitalist realism makes alienation feel inevitable rather than structural - if this is the only possible system, then feeling disconnected from your work is just how life is.
The spectacle replaces genuine connection with its representation - social media creates the appearance of community while often deepening alienation.
Alienation has been so thoroughly normalised that the question 'do you enjoy your work?' sounds naive rather than urgent.
Prolonged alienation can produce learned helplessness - the belief that meaninglessness is permanent and that nothing can be done about it.
Anchoring is a specific form of framing - the first number frames everything after it.
Anchoring is a specific form of framing - the first number frames everything after it.
Extreme anchors can shift what feels like a reasonable middle ground.
Extreme positions anchor the debate, making the merely unreasonable feel like a compromise.
An anchor can create a false dilemma by making only the options near it feel viable.
Anchoring interacts with loss aversion - once anchored to a high value, anything less feels like a loss.
Once anchored to a value, anything below it triggers loss aversion even if the lower value is objectively fair.
The most available number often becomes the anchor, regardless of whether it's the most relevant one.
Loaded language is the primary tool of appeal to emotion - choosing words that trigger feelings rather than convey facts.
Loaded language is the vehicle through which appeals to emotion are delivered - it's the mechanism that makes the appeal work.
Framing is how appeal to emotion is delivered - the same facts, framed emotionally, produce different conclusions.
An emotional appeal often functions as a red herring, diverting attention from the logical weakness of an argument.
Emotional appeals often serve as red herrings, generating strong feelings that divert attention from the logical weakness of the case.
Slippery slope arguments are powered by appeal to emotion - specifically, fear of an extreme outcome.
Slippery slope arguments work by triggering fear about an extreme outcome rather than addressing the actual proposal.
The halo effect explains why we trust attractive or successful people on topics they know nothing about - their positive qualities spill over into unrelated areas.
When a famous figure endorses something, their following can create a bandwagon - popularity substituting for evidence.
We're more likely to accept an authority figure's opinion when it confirms what we already believe.
Experts who venture outside their field may overestimate their competence in unfamiliar territory.
Authority figures provide a powerful form of social proof - if someone important believes it, it must be worth believing.
People who already prefer 'natural' products are more likely to notice and remember evidence that supports the natural-is-better assumption.
The word 'natural' functions as a halo - one positive association colours our judgement of everything else about the product or claim.
Labelling something 'natural' versus 'synthetic' frames identical information in ways that change people's responses.
The appeal to nature often creates a false choice between 'natural' and 'artificial' when most real options are a blend of both.
The appeal to nature works partly through emotion - 'natural' evokes warmth and safety, bypassing evidence-based evaluation.
When authority figures join the bandwagon, the effect intensifies - popularity plus authority feels like proof.
The Dunning-Kruger effect explains why we should be cautious about self-appointed authorities
Authority bias is one of the strongest forces that independent evaluation protects against
Negative events are more vivid and memorable, making them more available and therefore seeming more likely.
Negative events are more vivid and memorable, making them easier to recall and seemingly more common
Media framing determines which events become vivid and available - and therefore which risks we overestimate.
We're more likely to remember examples that confirm what we already believe, making those beliefs feel more justified than they are.
The availability heuristic and normalcy bias pull in opposite directions - one overestimates vivid risks, the other underestimates unfamiliar ones.
If a disaster has not happened recently or vividly, it feels less likely - reinforcing normalcy bias
Microaggressions are often the visible surface behaviour produced by the unconscious biases that aversive racism describes.
Microaggressions are often the visible surface of the unconscious biases that aversive racism describes.
Aversive racists may unconsciously seek information that confirms negative assumptions about certain groups while overlooking disconfirming evidence.
When evaluating behaviour, aversive racism can lead people to attribute the same action to character in some groups and to circumstance in others.
The tension between 'I am not prejudiced' and evidence of biased behaviour creates a dissonance that aversive racism resolves by keeping the bias unconscious.
Where aversive racism operates through unconscious individual bias, symbolic racism channels similar attitudes through political positions and policy opposition.
Aversive racism operates through unconscious individual bias in interpersonal settings; symbolic racism channels similar attitudes into political positions and policy preferences.
People selectively process new evidence to protect existing beliefs - the mechanism underlying the backfire effect
What happens when someone tries to correct a belief held through confirmation bias
The discomfort of holding contradictory beliefs drives people to reject the correction rather than update the belief
What happens when cognitive dissonance gets so intense that correction strengthens the original belief
People reason toward conclusions that protect their identity, not toward conclusions supported by evidence
The backfire effect is motivated reasoning under pressure - when challenged, people don't just maintain their beliefs, they strengthen them.
Overconfidence in one's existing knowledge makes corrections feel like attacks rather than helpful updates
The belief that you see reality clearly makes contradictory evidence feel like the other person's error, not yours
Social proof is the mechanism behind the bandwagon effect - we look to others' behaviour as evidence of what's correct.
The bandwagon effect is social proof at scale - when enough people adopt a behaviour, the social proof becomes self-reinforcing.
The bandwagon effect and pluralistic ignorance are mirror images - one is conforming to what everyone seems to believe, the other is assuming everyone believes something they don't.
When pluralistic ignorance makes a position seem universal, the bandwagon effect pushes even more people to publicly conform.
Once you've joined the bandwagon, confirmation bias keeps you on it - you notice evidence that supports the popular position and dismiss what contradicts it.
Popularity creates its own halo - we assume popular things must be good because so many people like them.
Popularity creates its own halo - we assume popular things must be good because so many people like them.
The Ben Franklin Effect is cognitive dissonance in action - we helped them, so we must like them
Betrayal aversion is a social variant of loss aversion - the pain of losing through betrayal is felt more acutely than equivalent loss from impersonal causes.
Being betrayed creates intense dissonance between your trust and the other person's behaviour, which is why it hurts more than equivalent harm from chance.
After a betrayal, we tend to attribute the cause to the other person's character rather than their circumstances, deepening the wound.
The emotional investment in a trusted relationship makes betrayal feel like a double loss - the harm itself plus all the trust you invested.
When betrayal is denied or reframed by the person who committed it, the combination of betrayal aversion and gaslighting creates compounded psychological harm.
Naive realism is the foundation that blind spot bias is built on - the belief that you see reality as it is.
The same gap between actual and perceived competence applies to self-awareness about bias - the less you see your own biases, the more confident you are that you don't have any.
Blind spot bias is often sustained by confirmation bias - you notice evidence that other people are biased and overlook evidence that you are too.
Projection can look like bias detection - attributing your own patterns to others while remaining unaware of them in yourself.
The motivation behind blind spot bias is ego protection - you reason your way to the conclusion that your thinking is clearer than everyone else's.
The attention economy is the modern infrastructure of bread and circuses - it captures and monetises the attention that might otherwise go to civic participation.
Bread and circuses and manufactured consent are complementary strategies - one distracts, the other shapes what you think about what's left.
Constant entertainment contributes to compassion fatigue by competing for the emotional energy that engagement with serious issues requires.
The bread-and-circuses dynamic normalises disengagement from civic life, making political apathy feel like a personal preference rather than a structural outcome.
Bread and circuses operate within capitalist realism - entertainment becomes the consolation prize for a system that can't be imagined differently.
When someone can't meet their burden of proof, they sometimes attack the questioner instead of providing evidence.
Misrepresenting a question about evidence as an attack on the claim itself is a common way to avoid the burden of proof.
We're less likely to demand evidence for claims that align with what we already believe - giving familiar ideas a free pass on the burden of proof.
Emotional appeals can substitute for evidence, making it feel like the burden of proof has been met when it hasn't.
When challenged to provide proof, the discomfort of not having it can trigger defensive responses instead of honest acknowledgement.
We look to the crowd to decide how to behave - and when the crowd does nothing, we follow suit.
The bystander effect is social proof in crisis - when nobody acts, the inaction itself becomes a signal that action isn't needed.
Everyone privately thinks something is wrong but publicly acts as if everything is fine, reinforcing collective inaction.
Pluralistic ignorance is one of the key mechanisms behind the bystander effect - everyone looks to the group and sees calm, so nobody acts.
After failing to act, people often rationalise their inaction to reduce the discomfort of knowing they could have helped.
Past good deeds can make people feel they've 'done enough,' reducing the urgency to step in again.
Separating what you witnessed from how you see yourself - 'I'm a good person, even though I didn't help.'
Capitalist realism is the current dominant form of cultural hegemony - the specific way power maintains itself as 'common sense' in market-driven societies
Capitalist realism defines the Overton window's deepest boundary - the point beyond which ideas aren't just unpopular but genuinely unthinkable
Manufactured consent describes the media mechanisms that help maintain capitalist realism - not through censorship but through the boundaries of 'reasonable' debate
Capitalist realism operates as a master frame - it determines the terms in which problems and solutions are even discussed
Capitalist realism is maintained by reinforcing feedback loops - the system produces the culture that produces the people who reproduce the system
Normalisation is the process through which capitalist realism maintains itself - each generation inherits the assumption rather than choosing it
Capitalist realism is the end state of a normalisation process so complete that the system feels like nature rather than a human construction
The Dunning-Kruger effect explains why people overestimate their competence - the circle of competence model is the antidote.
First principles reasoning is most reliable inside your circle of competence, where you know which principles actually hold.
Your probability estimates are only as good as your understanding of the domain - circle of competence determines the quality of those estimates.
Motivated reasoning tempts you to act outside your circle of competence when the potential reward is large enough.
Inside your circle, you can spot flawed reasoning. Outside it, confirmation bias fills the gap with false confidence.
Both fallacies distort what's being argued, but circular reasoning does it by looping back to the start rather than misrepresenting an opponent.
Confirmation bias makes circular reasoning feel satisfying - the conclusion confirms the belief you started with.
Another common logical fallacy that derails arguments by attacking the person rather than engaging with the claim.
Circular arguments often rely on emotional resonance to mask the fact that nothing has been proven.
Where circular reasoning loops back to the start, a red herring diverts attention away from the argument entirely.
The tool our brains use to resolve cognitive dissonance without actually changing our minds
Motivated reasoning is one of the primary tools for resolving cognitive dissonance - it constructs justifications that reduce the uncomfortable gap.
We avoid dissonance by seeking information that confirms what we already believe
Projection is a way of offloading the discomfort of cognitive dissonance - attributing the unwanted trait to someone else rather than facing it in yourself.
Normalisation and collective amnesia work together - once something has been normalised, the history of how it became normal is forgotten.
When the true history is forgotten, the replacement narrative benefits from the illusory truth effect - repeated often enough, it becomes the accepted story.
Collective amnesia exploits the availability heuristic - if you can't easily recall a historical pattern, it doesn't factor into your assessment of the present.
How history is framed determines what's remembered and what's forgotten - framing is the editorial mechanism of collective amnesia.
The dominant culture controls which histories are told, taught, and preserved - collective amnesia is one of hegemony's most powerful tools.
Capitalist realism creates the conditions under which commodification feels natural - if the market is the only real system, then everything must eventually become a market product
Commodification is one of the key mechanisms through which cultural hegemony maintains itself - by making market logic feel like the only logic
Recuperation is what happens when commodification targets dissent itself - rebellion becomes a product, protest becomes a brand
Recuperation is a specific form of commodification - the commodification of dissent, where opposition itself becomes the product
Conceptual gentrification is the commodification of ideas specifically - stripping concepts of their radical edge and repackaging them for the market
Conceptual gentrification is a specific form of commodification - the commodification of ideas, where intellectual challenge is converted into consumable content
Commodification works partly through framing - redefining experiences in market terms changes how we value them
Social proof accelerates commodification - when everyone treats something as a product, treating it as anything else feels naive
Compartmentalisation is a strategy for avoiding cognitive dissonance - keeping conflicting beliefs apart so the discomfort never arises.
Where denial blocks a truth entirely, compartmentalisation acknowledges the truth but isolates it from anything it might contradict.
Compartmentalisation keeps conflicting truths in separate mental boxes; denial refuses to let the truth in at all.
Rationalisation constructs a story to make contradictions feel acceptable; compartmentalisation avoids the contradiction altogether by keeping the beliefs separate.
Moral licensing lets people act badly after acting well - compartmentalisation is the mechanism that prevents the two from being weighed together.
Motivated reasoning operates within each compartment, ensuring that the beliefs inside it feel internally consistent.
Constant media coverage makes threats feel more frequent than they are, accelerating emotional exhaustion.
The pain of watching others suffer without being able to help can feel like a personal loss, draining emotional reserves.
The tension between wanting to help and feeling unable to creates discomfort that compassion fatigue numbs away.
When millions see the same crisis, each person feels less personally responsible - and compassion fatigue makes it easier to let that feeling win.
How suffering is presented to us - statistics vs. individual stories - dramatically affects whether compassion fatigue takes hold.
Recuperation absorbs radical movements as products; conceptual gentrification absorbs radical ideas as comfortable content - two faces of the same mechanism
Conceptual gentrification is recuperation applied to ideas and frameworks - stripping them of their radical edge and repackaging them for comfortable consumption
Conceptual gentrification works by reframing ideas - shifting the frame from challenging to comfortable while keeping the name intact
Capitalist realism drives conceptual gentrification by ensuring that only market-compatible versions of ideas survive mainstream circulation
Conceptual gentrification is one way cultural hegemony maintains itself - by allowing challenging ideas to circulate only in defanged form
The gentrified version of an idea spreads so widely that it becomes 'the' version - repetition replaces the original through sheer familiarity
Concern trolling often overlaps with tone policing - both shift focus from substance to style.
Concern trolling and tone policing both use the appearance of helpfulness to silence opposition.
Where concern trolling hides behind fake sympathy, sealioning hides behind fake curiosity.
Where concern trolling hides hostility behind fake sympathy, sealioning hides it behind fake curiosity.
Both tactics make the target question their own judgement, but gaslighting is more personal and sustained.
Concern trolls sometimes build straw man versions of a cause to make it easier to criticise.
Understanding motivated reasoning helps explain why concern trolls seem so convinced by their own performance.
The engine behind confirmation bias - we use our intelligence to defend conclusions we've already reached
Confirmation bias is the search filter; motivated reasoning is the defence lawyer. One finds the evidence, the other argues the case.
Repeated exposure to confirming information makes it feel even more true
Once the illusory truth effect makes a claim feel credible, confirmation bias locks it in by filtering for supporting evidence.
The first piece of information you encounter sets a reference point - a specific form of contrast
How something is presented shapes your perception of it, often through implied contrast
Losses feel larger when contrasted with recent gains, amplifying the pain
A positive first impression creates a favourable contrast for everything that follows
Recent comparisons are easier to recall and therefore more likely to shape your current judgement
Status quo bias is the psychological mechanism that keeps cultural defaults in place - we prefer the current arrangement simply because it's current.
Cultural defaults are one of the primary vehicles through which hegemony operates - the dominant culture's preferences become the invisible standard.
Normalisation is the process by which a cultural default is created - something is repeated often enough that it stops being noticed.
Cultural defaults frame certain choices as normal and others as deviations, shaping decisions before any conscious evaluation begins.
Naive realism makes cultural defaults invisible - if you believe you see reality as it is, the defaults feel like nature rather than culture.
Chomsky's model describes a specific mechanism through which hegemony operates - media as a system that produces consent for the status quo
Hegemony works partly through framing - determining not just what people think about, but the terms in which they think about it
The Overton window is hegemony made visible - the boundaries of 'acceptable' opinion are set by whoever holds cultural power
Hegemonic ideas spread partly through social proof - when everyone around you accepts something, it feels true
Naive realism is what hegemony feels like from the inside - you're not following an ideology, you're just seeing the world as it is
Normalisation is hegemony's engine - the slow process by which constructed ideas come to feel natural and inevitable
Normalisation is the engine of cultural hegemony - it's the process by which the dominant group's ideas stop being seen as ideas at all and become 'just how things are'
DARVO and gaslighting often appear together - both make the target doubt their own experience.
DARVO is a specific gaslighting sequence - deny, attack, then reverse victim and offender.
The 'attack' phase of DARVO often takes the form of tone policing the accuser's emotions.
Tone policing often appears during the 'attack' phase of DARVO, when the accused criticises how the accuser raised the issue.
DARVO exploits the discomfort people feel when someone they trusted is accused of harm.
The 'attack' step in DARVO is a textbook ad hominem - targeting the person rather than addressing the claim.
Both tactics use performance to disguise intent, though DARVO is more aggressive.
Denial is one way people avoid the discomfort of holding contradictory beliefs.
Where denial refuses to see the truth, rationalisation invents a story to make it acceptable.
Where denial refuses to see the truth, rationalisation sees it but constructs a story to make it feel acceptable.
Motivated reasoning is the thinking process that supports denial - seeking only the evidence that confirms what you want to believe.
Denial is motivated reasoning taken to its extreme - refusing to acknowledge evidence that threatens a core belief or identity.
Confirmation bias helps sustain denial by filtering out contradictory information.
The bystander effect is the most famous expression of diffusion of responsibility - the more witnesses to an emergency, the less likely any one is to help.
When nobody acts, each individual assumes the situation must not be serious - pluralistic ignorance and diffusion of responsibility reinforce each other.
Shared resources degrade because each individual assumes their contribution to the problem is too small to matter - a structural form of diffusion.
Believing you've already done your part (signed a petition, shared a post) can diffuse your sense of further responsibility.
Repeated experiences of diffused responsibility can produce learned helplessness - the belief that individual action is pointless.
Loaded language works on everyone's emotions openly; dog whistles work on a specific group's understanding covertly.
Dog whistles are a form of framing - choosing specific words to activate specific associations.
Dog whistles create in-group recognition, reinforcing belonging through shared coded understanding.
Dog whistling is one mechanism through which public opinion can be shaped without open debate.
Dog whistles bypass rational argument and appeal directly to emotional and tribal associations.
Effort justification is one of the primary ways we resolve cognitive dissonance - if the experience was painful but we chose it, we inflate its value to make the choice feel rational.
Sunk costs and effort justification reinforce each other - the more effort you've invested, the more you justify it, and the more justified it feels, the harder it is to walk away.
Effort justification is a specific form of rationalisation - constructing a story about value that runs backwards from the effort rather than forwards from the outcome.
Motivated reasoning provides the intellectual scaffolding for effort justification - we selectively find evidence that the hard experience was worthwhile.
Having endured difficulty can feel like moral credit - licensing us to dismiss those who found an easier path.
Emergent behaviours often arise from feedback loops operating between many individual agents.
Feedback loops between many agents are one of the key drivers of emergence - complex patterns arising from simple interactions.
Emergence is the systemic cousin of unintended consequences - complex outcomes nobody planned.
Unintended consequences arise because complex systems produce emergent behaviour that resists prediction.
Social norms can emerge from pluralistic ignorance - everyone follows a rule that nobody privately endorses.
Trends and movements can emerge from individual bandwagon decisions cascading through a population.
The tragedy of the commons is an emergent outcome - collective destruction arising from individually rational choices.
The tragedy is an emergent outcome - no one intends the collapse, but it arises from the interaction of individually rational choices.
Loaded language amplifies emotional charge; euphemism does the opposite - both manipulate how we feel about facts.
Euphemism and loaded language are two sides of the same coin - one softens emotional impact, the other amplifies it.
Euphemism is a specific framing technique - it frames harsh realities in softer terms to change how we respond.
Euphemism is one of the key linguistic tools through which public consent for unpopular actions is manufactured.
When euphemism is used deliberately to make someone doubt the severity of what happened, it crosses into gaslighting territory.
Both shut down deeper thinking - euphemism by softening reality, thought-terminating clichés by dismissing it.
Both soften engagement with difficult realities - euphemism by changing the words, thought-terminating clichés by ending the conversation.
The frame through which we view someone sets the expectations they can then violate - framing and expectancy violation work as a pair.
The halo effect sets positive expectations that, when violated, produce especially strong negative reactions.
When expectations are violated, we tend to attribute the violation to the person's character rather than their circumstances.
An expectancy violation creates dissonance between what you believed about someone and what they just did.
First impressions set anchors that become the baseline expectations against which all subsequent behaviour is measured.
False balance is the media-facing version of false equivalence - treating unequal things as equal, applied specifically to editorial and journalistic framing.
False balance is the media-specific expression of false equivalence - giving equal airtime to unequal positions.
False balance is a framing choice. Presenting two sides as equal frames the issue as genuinely contested, even when it isn't.
False balance is a framing choice - presenting two unequal positions as though they deserve equal consideration.
False balance can be manufactured deliberately by interest groups who fund contrarian voices to create the appearance of scientific debate.
False balance is one mechanism through which consent is manufactured - by framing elite-friendly positions as the 'balanced' centre.
False balance feeds confirmation bias - it gives people who want to disbelieve the evidence a seemingly credible alternative to point to.
When both sides of a non-debate are given equal airtime, the minority position becomes more mentally 'available' than its actual support warrants.
Capitalist realism is the environment in which false consciousness thrives - when no alternative system is imaginable, supporting the current one feels like realism rather than ideology.
Cultural hegemony is the mechanism through which false consciousness is produced - the dominant culture's values become common sense.
Motivated reasoning is the psychological process that sustains false consciousness - people construct arguments to defend conclusions they've already absorbed from the culture.
False consciousness is maintained by framing - when issues are presented in certain terms, conclusions that serve power feel like personal convictions.
Once false consciousness is established, confirmation bias reinforces it - people seek out information that confirms the worldview they've absorbed.
Confirmation bias filters what we notice; the false consensus effect filters what we assume about everyone else.
Two sides of the same coin - false consensus makes us think everyone agrees with us, pluralistic ignorance makes us think everyone agrees with each other.
The false consensus effect can create phantom bandwagons - we assume the crowd already agrees, so we never check.
We estimate what 'most people' think based on the examples most available to us - which are usually people like us.
We spend more time with people who share our views, which inflates our sense of how common those views are.
Naive realism says 'I see the world as it is' - the false consensus effect adds 'and so does everyone else.'
Both fallacies simplify an argument to make it easier to win - straw man distorts the opponent's position, false dilemma eliminates alternatives.
Often used together - straw man the opposing view, then present a false choice
How options are presented changes which one people choose. False dilemmas are an extreme form of framing that removes options entirely.
Often paired with false dilemmas - 'either we stop this now or it leads to catastrophe' combines both fallacies.
Slippery slope often hides a false dilemma - either we allow nothing, or we lose everything.
False dilemmas frequently rely on fear or urgency to make the binary choice feel unavoidable.
Where false dilemma reduces options to two, false equivalence treats two things as equal when they aren't.
Both fallacies distort the landscape of a debate - false dilemma collapses it to two options, false equivalence inflates a weak option to match a strong one.
False equivalence often shifts the burden of proof - forcing the well-evidenced position to defend itself against unsupported claims.
Whataboutism creates false equivalence by deflecting criticism with an unrelated counter-accusation.
Whataboutism creates false equivalence by placing two unrelated wrongs side by side, implying they cancel each other out.
Citing a single dissenting expert against a consensus of thousands is a common route to false equivalence.
Feedback loops are what you find when you follow second-order thinking far enough - effects producing effects producing effects.
Second-order effects often create feedback loops that amplify or dampen the original action.
The illusory truth effect is a cognitive feedback loop - repetition breeds belief, which breeds more repetition.
Social popularity is a classic reinforcing loop - the more people join, the more others want to join.
Commons collapse often involves a reinforcing loop - as the resource degrades, competition for what's left intensifies.
Commons collapse often involves a reinforcing loop - as the resource degrades, competition for what's left intensifies, accelerating the degradation.
The Gish Gallop is the debate-stage version of the firehose - overwhelming an opponent with too many claims to address.
The firehose of falsehood is the Gish Gallop scaled up from a debate to an entire media ecosystem.
Whataboutism is a common tool within a firehose strategy - deflecting every accusation with a counter-accusation.
Each false claim in a firehose functions as a red herring, pulling attention away from what matters.
The firehose works partly because people will latch onto the claims that confirm what they already believe.
The sheer volume of repeated claims makes them feel more plausible through the availability heuristic.
Inversion flips problems around; first principles strips them down - both escape conventional thinking.
First principles builds up from truths; inversion clears the path by identifying what to avoid.
First principles tells you what's true; second-order thinking tells you what happens next.
First principles tells you what's true; second-order thinking tells you what happens next.
Reminds you that models and assumptions are simplifications, not reality itself.
First principles thinking builds from foundational truths, but even those truths are models - this concept provides the warning label.
Both favour simplicity - Occam's Razor in explanations, first principles in reasoning.
Both favour stripping problems back to fundamentals rather than accepting layered assumptions.
Returning to first principles can trigger discomfort when foundational truths clash with existing beliefs.
Loaded language is a framing tool - choosing emotionally charged words over neutral ones to shape the audience's response.
Loaded language is one of the primary tools of framing - it shapes how a fact feels before you've had time to think about it.
Loss framing is more powerful than gain framing because we feel losses roughly twice as strongly as equivalent gains.
Loss framing is more powerful than gain framing precisely because of loss aversion.
The belief that you see reality as it truly is - making other people's different behaviour seem irrational rather than differently situated
Naive realism makes you attribute other people's different views to character flaws rather than different circumstances
Once you attribute positive character traits to someone, you interpret all their behaviour through that lens
The halo effect feeds attribution errors - we explain likeable people's failures as bad luck and unlikeable people's successes as flukes.
When your actions contradict your self-image, you attribute the mismatch to circumstances - a luxury you rarely extend to others
Once you have decided someone is a certain type of person, you notice evidence that confirms it
Attacking a person's character rather than their argument - often fuelled by the fundamental attribution error
Tone policing can function as soft gaslighting - redirecting attention from what someone said to how they said it.
Denial is the psychological defence that gaslighters weaponise - denying that events happened, that words were said, or that harm was done.
Gaslighting creates intense cognitive dissonance - the gap between what you experienced and what you're being told you experienced.
Victims of gaslighting may engage in motivated reasoning to preserve the relationship, finding reasons to believe the gaslighter over their own memory.
Many individual claims in a Gish Gallop function as red herrings - distractions from the strongest arguments.
Gish Gallop arguments often include straw man distortions that are quicker to state than to correct.
The Gish Gallop shifts the burden of proof by forcing the opponent to debunk rather than the galloper to support.
The first claims in a Gish Gallop can anchor the audience's thinking before the opponent has a chance to respond.
The halo effect explains part of why authority bias works - we assume competence in one domain extends to others.
Once the halo is in place, confirmation bias keeps it there - we notice evidence that supports our positive impression and ignore what contradicts it.
The halo effect is a specific instance of the affect heuristic - our feelings about one trait spill over into our judgement of everything else.
The tendency to blame people's character rather than their circumstances - the exact error Hanlon's Razor corrects.
When someone's behaviour clashes with your expectations, Hanlon's Razor helps you avoid jumping to the worst explanation.
Both razors favour simpler explanations - Occam's for theories, Hanlon's for motives.
Both razors shave away unnecessary complexity - Occam's in explanations, Hanlon's in motives.
Once you decide someone acted maliciously, confirmation bias makes you see malice everywhere.
Hanlon's Razor has limits - some behaviour really is malicious, and recognising that boundary matters.
We generalise most readily from examples that confirm what we already believe, ignoring the ones that don't fit.
Generalising from visible successes while missing the invisible failures is a specific form of hasty generalisation.
Drawing lessons from survivors alone is a hasty generalisation - the sample is not representative because it excludes everyone who failed.
The examples that come to mind most easily feel more representative than they are, fuelling overgeneralisation.
Early examples anchor our thinking, making later examples feel like confirmation rather than new data.
Hasty generalisations about an opponent's views are often the raw material for straw man arguments.
Hindsight bias makes success stories seem inevitable by hiding the role of luck and the failures that did not survive
After an outcome, people selectively remember the evidence that pointed toward it and forget the evidence that did not
The false confidence that comes from hindsight bias can mimic the overconfidence of the Dunning-Kruger effect
How an outcome is described after the fact shapes how inevitable it seems
Recent and vivid outcomes are easier to recall, making them feel more predictable in retrospect
The first version of a claim you hear sets an anchor that subsequent repetitions reinforce.
How a repeated claim is framed - which words are used, what's emphasised - shapes which version of 'truth' takes hold.
When repeated claims also appear popular, the combination of familiarity and social proof becomes very difficult to resist.
We're especially vulnerable to the illusory truth effect when the repeated claim aligns with what we already want to believe.
Believing your group sees reality clearly while the out-group is biased
Out-group members' behaviour is attributed to character; in-group members get the benefit of context
Group identity drives conformity - people adopt beliefs to maintain belonging
People look to their in-group for cues about what to believe and how to behave
You seek evidence that your group is right and the other group is wrong
Other people's opinions act as anchors - independent evaluation means forming your view before the anchor lands.
Social proof pulls you toward the crowd's judgement. Independent evaluation asks you to step back from it.
The bandwagon effect is what happens when independent evaluation fails - people adopt popular positions without scrutiny.
Even independent evaluations can be distorted by confirmation bias if you approach them with a preexisting conclusion.
First principles thinking builds knowledge from the ground up; independent evaluation applies that same independence to judging claims and ideas.
Inversion identifies hazards; second-order thinking traces how those hazards unfold.
Inversion asks 'what could go wrong?' - second-order thinking maps out how it could go wrong, step by step.
Inversion surfaces risks that probabilistic thinking can then assess for likelihood and severity.
Inversion works with loss aversion rather than against it - your brain is already tuned to spot threats.
Survivorship bias hides the failure cases that inversion is specifically designed to reveal.
Both fallacies overemphasise personal responsibility and underestimate the power of circumstances - the just-world fallacy adds a moral dimension.
We see the people who succeeded and assume they deserved it, while the equally deserving people who didn't succeed remain invisible.
Once we believe the world is fair, we notice evidence that confirms it and overlook evidence of unfairness.
Witnessing innocent suffering creates discomfort that the just-world fallacy resolves - by redefining the sufferer as not truly innocent.
Success creates a halo that makes people seem more deserving, reinforcing the just-world belief.
Learned helplessness reduces cognitive dissonance by aligning expectations with outcomes: if you expect failure, failure doesn't feel contradictory.
Sometimes what looks like learned helplessness is actually the sunk cost fallacy in disguise - staying in a bad situation because of what's already been invested.
Once learned helplessness sets in, confirmation bias filters for evidence that supports the belief that nothing can change.
How a setback is framed - as a learning experience or as proof of inability - determines whether helplessness takes hold.
Believing 'I can't change this' can become motivated - it protects against the pain of trying and failing again.
Loaded language is especially effective on people who already lean a particular way - it confirms and intensifies existing feelings.
When someone uses loaded language to describe your reasonable behaviour ('You're being hysterical'), it can cross into gaslighting.
Loss aversion fuels the sunk cost fallacy - walking away means accepting the loss of everything already invested.
Accepting a loss creates dissonance with our self-image as good decision-makers, which makes the loss feel even worse.
Loss aversion and negativity bias share a root - the brain gives more weight to negative experiences than positive ones.
The pain of losing something outweighs the pleasure of gaining the same thing - a specific expression of negativity bias
The lump of labour fallacy is a false dilemma in disguise - it presents employment as either-or when the reality is both-and.
If you assume there's a fixed amount of work, it becomes easy to blame newcomers for taking what 'should' belong to others.
The fallacy often relies on anecdotal evidence from a single sector, generalised to the entire economy.
Whether immigration is framed as 'competition for jobs' or 'expansion of the workforce' entirely changes how people evaluate it.
The lump of labour argument works by triggering fear of loss rather than presenting economic evidence.
Manufactured consent operates largely through framing - not lying about events, but controlling how they're presented and which ones matter.
Manufactured consent helps define the Overton Window - the range of ideas that feel acceptable is partly shaped by what media treats as serious.
Manufactured consent helps define where the window sits - the media's structural filters shape which ideas feel acceptable.
Two different models of propaganda - manufactured consent works through omission and framing, the firehose works through overwhelming volume.
Repeated framing across multiple outlets triggers the illusory truth effect - the consensus feels earned when it's actually structural.
Manufactured desire relies heavily on social proof - showing you that other people already want the thing makes you want it too.
The bandwagon effect is one of the key mechanisms through which manufactured desire spreads - wanting what everyone else seems to want.
Manufacturing desire is the demand side of commodification - once something is turned into a product, desire for it must be created.
The attention economy provides the infrastructure through which manufactured desire is delivered - capturing attention is the first step in creating want.
Manufactured desire works by framing products as solutions to problems you didn't know you had.
Probabilistic models are among the most useful maps, but they are still maps - subject to the same limitations.
Every map frames reality in a particular way, emphasising some features and hiding others.
Our mental maps are disproportionately shaped by whatever information is most vivid or recent.
Occam's Razor favours simpler models - this concept reminds you that even the best simple model is still a simplification.
The illusory truth effect is what happens when mere exposure meets claims and statements - repetition makes things feel true, not just familiar.
Things we've been exposed to more are easier to recall, which makes them seem more important or common.
Repeated exposure to an idea makes it feel popular, which triggers the bandwagon effect and creates a self-reinforcing cycle.
We seek out what's familiar, and the mere exposure effect rewards us for doing so - each strengthens the other.
Repeated exposure to a particular framing makes that frame feel like the natural way to understand an issue.
Where dog whistling is public and strategic, microaggressions are personal and usually unconscious - but both rely on coded communication.
The tribal instinct that makes us unconsciously treat 'outsiders' differently in small, everyday ways.
People on the receiving end sometimes normalise microaggressions because they are so constant - and people committing them normalise their own behaviour for the same reason.
Bystanders often stay silent during microaggressions because they assume nobody else noticed or minded.
Betrayal aversion is about broken trust. Moral hypocrisy judgement is about broken consistency between values and actions.
Projection and moral hypocrisy often travel together - the loudest moralisers are sometimes projecting their own failings
The hypocrite is experiencing cognitive dissonance between their stated values and their behaviour. We're experiencing it too, when we discover the gap.
Moral hypocrisy avoids the dissonance that would arise from applying the same standards to yourself that you demand of others.
Compartmentalisation is the mechanism that makes moral hypocrisy possible - keeping your judgement of others and your assessment of yourself in separate mental boxes.
Moral licensing gives people permission to behave badly after behaving well; moral hypocrisy lets them judge others for the same behaviour.
The fundamental attribution error judges others by their actions and yourself by your intentions - the exact asymmetry that powers moral hypocrisy.
Rationalisation provides the internal story that explains why the standard that applies to others does not apply to you.
Moral licensing is one way the brain resolves dissonance - the good deed provides cover for the inconsistency.
After earning moral credits, the brain becomes skilled at generating reasons why the subsequent behaviour is acceptable.
Moral licensing often surfaces as rationalisation: 'I've done my bit, so this exception is fine.'
A single good quality or action casts a glow over everything else, making subsequent bad behaviour easier to excuse.
When moral licensing is widespread in a group, it can become normalised - everyone does a token good thing and then collectively relaxes.
Moral panics exploit the availability heuristic - intense media coverage makes the threat feel more common and immediate than it is.
Every moral panic needs a villain. Scapegoating provides one - a group that can be blamed for the perceived threat.
Moral panics are sustained by emotional appeal - fear, outrage, and disgust rather than evidence and proportion.
Moral panics can serve the same function as manufactured consent - aligning public attention with certain interests.
The language of moral panics is always loaded - 'epidemic', 'invasion', 'crisis' - words chosen to escalate rather than inform.
Rationalisation is the output of motivated reasoning - the plausible-sounding story you construct after the conclusion has already been reached.
Motivated reasoning is the engine that powers rationalisation - the process of seeking evidence to support a predetermined conclusion.
The straw man attacks a weaker version of an opponent's argument; the motte-and-bailey defends a weaker version of your own.
The motte-and-bailey is a kind of reverse straw man - distorting your own position rather than your opponent's
Both tactics involve shifting the terms of the argument mid-conversation, but in different directions.
The retreat phase of a motte-and-bailey can feel like gaslighting - 'That's not what I said' when it clearly was.
The motte-and-bailey works by treating two very different claims as equivalent.
Audiences often experience cognitive dissonance when the bold claim and the defended claim don't match.
Moving the goalposts is often a response to someone meeting their burden of proof - the goalposts move precisely because the evidence was convincing.
When goalpost-moving fails, the next move is often to attack the person presenting the evidence rather than engaging with it.
Both tactics avoid engaging with the actual argument - straw man by distorting it, goalpost-moving by changing the criteria.
Moving the goalposts is often a way to avoid the discomfort of admitting you were wrong - each new demand buys time against the growing evidence.
Moving the goalposts can function as a diversion - the new demand redirects attention away from the fact that the original demand was satisfied.
Moving the goalposts and red herring are both evasion tactics, but they operate differently - one shifts the standard, the other shifts the subject.
You selectively seek evidence that confirms the view you already believe to be objectively true
Overconfidence in your own perception shares structural similarities with overconfidence in your own competence
Naive realism can make people reject any comparison between their view and another, treating equivalence itself as offensive
Encountering someone who sees reality differently creates discomfort that naive realism resolves by dismissing their perspective
Negative framing triggers stronger reactions than equivalent positive framing
Negativity bias can make people selectively notice information that confirms a pessimistic worldview
The opposite distortion - focusing only on what succeeded while ignoring failures
People selectively notice evidence that things are fine and dismiss evidence that something is changing
Accepting that a familiar situation has become dangerous creates discomfort that normalcy bias resolves
The potential losses from acknowledging a threat feel more real than the abstract threat itself
People are motivated to conclude that things are fine because the alternative is frightening
The Overton window moves through normalisation - each small shift in what's acceptable resets the boundaries of mainstream debate
The illusory truth effect is normalisation's psychological mechanism - the more you hear something, the more normal it feels, regardless of whether it's true
Social proof accelerates normalisation - when everyone around you accepts something, your threshold for accepting it drops
Framing and normalisation work together - how something is framed determines whether it registers as alarming or ordinary
People often reject simpler explanations because they don't fit what they already believe.
The desire for a particular conclusion can make complex, unlikely explanations feel more convincing than simple ones.
Occam's Razor is closely related to probabilistic reasoning - simpler theories tend to have higher prior probability.
Simpler explanations tend to have higher prior probability - Occam's Razor is probabilistic thinking applied to theories.
The sunk cost fallacy ignores opportunity cost - it keeps you invested in past choices instead of evaluating current alternatives.
Second-order thinking helps you trace not just what you chose, but what the consequences of not choosing the alternative would be.
Loss aversion makes opportunity costs feel less real than direct costs, which is why we consistently underweight them.
First principles thinking helps you evaluate opportunities on their actual merits rather than on habit or convention.
Inversion asks what you would regret not doing - which is opportunity cost viewed from the perspective of future you.
Framing is one of the primary tools for shifting the window - how an idea is presented determines whether it feels radical or reasonable.
We assume the current window is stable, making us slow to recognise when it's shifting.
The paradox of tolerance is about what happens at the window's edges - when intolerant ideas get normalised into the range of acceptable debate.
The paradox of tolerance is about what happens at the edges of the Overton Window - when intolerant ideas are normalised into the range of acceptable debate.
False balance can enable the paradox - giving extremist positions equal airtime in the name of fairness helps normalise them.
The paradox is often misused as a false dilemma: 'either we tolerate everything or we censor everything.' Popper's point was more nuanced.
Intolerant movements often use motte-and-bailey tactics - defending extreme positions by retreating to reasonable-sounding versions when challenged.
Critics of the paradox often invoke slippery slope arguments - claiming that any limits on tolerance will inevitably lead to total censorship.
We use others' behaviour as evidence of what's normal and correct, which means collective silence gets misread as collective agreement.
Pluralistic ignorance is social proof gone wrong - everyone follows the group, but nobody in the group actually agrees.
Once you believe everyone else agrees with a norm, you notice the people who follow it and overlook those who quietly resist.
Publicly going along with something you privately reject creates dissonance - which is often resolved by adjusting your private beliefs to match your public behaviour.
We're more likely to notice and remember sequences that confirm what we already believe, reinforcing post hoc thinking.
Vivid or recent events feel more significant, making coincidental timing seem more meaningful than it is.
Once we've committed to a causal explanation, contradictory evidence creates discomfort we're motivated to resolve - often by doubling down.
How a sequence of events is described can make a causal link seem obvious or invisible.
We're drawn to causal explanations that support what we want to believe, making post hoc reasoning harder to resist.
We overweight vivid or recent events, which distorts our intuitive sense of how likely things are.
Probabilistic thinking requires updating beliefs with new evidence - confirmation bias resists exactly that.
Probabilistic thinking estimates what might happen; second-order thinking follows the chain of consequences.
Loss aversion distorts probability judgements by making potential losses loom larger than equivalent gains.
Projection can become gaslighting when used deliberately to make someone question their own perception of reality.
Projection builds on denial - you first refuse to see the trait in yourself, then locate it in someone else.
Both involve misjudging the source of behaviour, but projection is about your own traits being misattributed to others.
Naive realism assumes you see the world as it is - projection distorts what you see in others to match what you cannot face in yourself.
Rationalisation is one of the primary tools people use to resolve cognitive dissonance without changing their behaviour.
Confirmation bias provides the raw material for rationalisation by filtering which evidence you notice.
Rationalisation is often the mechanism behind sunk cost reasoning - inventing reasons to continue with a failing investment.
Recuperation is one of the key mechanisms that maintains capitalist realism - by absorbing every critique, the system demonstrates that nothing exists outside it
Recuperation is how cultural hegemony renews itself - by digesting challenges rather than fighting them, the dominant culture stays dominant
While manufactured consent manages dissent through media filters, recuperation manages it by converting dissent into a consumer experience
Recuperation works by reframing radical ideas - shifting them from a threat to the system into a product of the system
Whataboutism is a specific type of red herring - diverting from the issue at hand by pointing to someone else's wrongdoing.
Whataboutism is a specific type of red herring - it diverts from the issue by introducing a different accusation.
Straw man and red herring both involve shifting the argument, but straw man distorts the original point while red herring abandons it entirely.
Reification is one of hegemony's most powerful tools - when the dominant arrangement feels like nature rather than choice, challenging it becomes almost unthinkable.
Normalisation is the process through which reification happens - something is repeated until it stops being noticed, and then it starts being treated as permanent.
Status quo bias is the psychological complement to reification - we prefer what already exists, and reification makes what exists feel like the only possibility.
Capitalist realism is reification applied to an entire economic system - the inability to imagine alternatives makes the current arrangement feel like a fact of nature.
Reification confuses the map for the territory - treating our models of reality as if they were reality itself.
Zero-sum thinking is the cognitive frame that makes relative deprivation feel rational - if someone else is gaining, I must be losing.
Relative deprivation is what zero-sum thinking feels like from the inside - the sense that your position is slipping even when your absolute situation hasn't changed.
Losing relative position activates the same psychology as losing something tangible - it hurts more than an equivalent gain in absolute terms would help.
Relative deprivation hits hardest when the comparison is with an out-group - 'they' are getting what 'we' deserve.
Relative deprivation depends entirely on which comparison is made salient - change the reference point and the same situation feels like comfort or injustice.
Social media and news make upward comparisons constantly available, amplifying relative deprivation far beyond what we'd experience in daily life.
Media and political systems can manufacture relative deprivation by choosing which comparisons to put in front of people.
Scapegoating is ad hominem at scale - attacking a person or group rather than addressing the actual problem.
Scapegoating functions as a red herring, diverting attention from root causes to a more emotionally satisfying target.
Scapegoating reframes complex systemic problems as simple stories with clear villains.
Scapegoating relies on the tendency to blame individuals or groups for outcomes that are shaped by systems and circumstances.
Scapegoating is almost always accompanied by loaded language - emotionally charged words that dehumanise the target and justify the blame.
Sealioning weaponises the burden of proof by endlessly demanding evidence while offering none.
Both tactics exploit the asymmetry between making claims and responding to them, but in opposite directions.
Sealioners often deploy tone policing when their target gets frustrated - 'I was being perfectly civil.'
Sealioners often deploy tone policing when their target becomes frustrated by the relentless questioning.
The sealioner's insistence that they are being reasonable can make the target doubt their own perception of the interaction.
Unintended consequences are often the result of failing to apply second-order thinking.
